summary: This notebook delivers a comprehensive exploratory data analysis (EDA) of the NFL Big Data Bowl 2026 tracking dataset, focusing specifically on pass plays. It loads and merges pre-throw and in-air player tracking frames with supplementary metadata, then performs statistical profiling, distribution analysis, and spatial-temporal visualization. Key techniques include calculating receiver-defender separation metrics, validating tracking data against human physics limits, and generating interactive animations and 3D scatter plots to illustrate player movement patterns. Readers should understand how to structure frame-by-frame tracking data, identify spatial-temporal features relevant to pass prediction, and recognize data quality constraints like speed and acceleration bounds.
competition_flow: Loads weekly tracking CSVs and supplementary metadata, merges them by game/play/player, computes spatial-temporal metrics like separation and speed distributions, and visualizes the data through interactive charts and animations to understand pass play dynamics.
data_reading: Reads weekly `input_2023_w{week:02}.csv` and `output_2023_w{week:02}.csv` files via `pandas.read_csv`, concatenates them across weeks, and merges with `supplementary_data.csv` on `game_id` and `play_id`.
data_processing: Sorts merged frames by game/play/frame, filters by player position/role, and applies physics-based validation checks for speed and acceleration bounds.
features_engineering: Computes receiver-defender separation metrics (nearest distance, defenders within 3 yards, average distance), derives throw time from frame counts, estimates pass distance from ball landing coordinates, and calculates displacement/speed/acceleration statistics by role.
insights: Receiver separation and spatial positioning during the in-air phase are primary drivers of pass play outcomes.
Frame-by-frame tracking data can be effectively structured and visualized using spatial-temporal plots to reveal strategic movement patterns.
Player roles and positions fundamentally dictate speed distributions, acceleration profiles, and separation dynamics.
Merging pre-throw and in-air tracking data with supplementary metadata enables comprehensive play-level context for feature derivation.
critical_findings: Tracking data strictly adheres to human physics limits, with speed and acceleration violations being negligible.
Inside linebackers exhibit late-air acceleration bursts that close separation gaps, impacting receiver targeting windows.
